flashcards.Back: The first phase of an ambulance run involving the inspection of vehicle systems, medical equipment, and personnel readiness.
The phase involving a central access point where trained personnel collect and transmit essential emergency call information.
The phase involving safe emergency vehicle driving, seat belt use, and notifying dispatch while proceeding to the scene.
The phase where EMTs park safely, notify dispatch, perform a scene size-up, and establish body substance isolation.
The phase where the patient is safely lifted, moved, and secured into the ambulance using appropriate carrying devices.
The phase during which the EMT continues ongoing assessment, monitors vital signs, and communicates with the receiving hospital.
The phase where the EMT transfers patient care, providing both a concise verbal report and a completed written report.
The phase involving the cleaning and disinfection of the ambulance and restocking of disposable supplies.
The final phase of an ambulance call where the unit is refueled, reports are filed, and complete cleaning occurs.
The process of sorting multiple casualties into specific priorities for emergency medical care and transportation.
Triage category for patients with airway and breathing difficulties, severe bleeding, shock, or decreased mental status.
The triage priority assigned to patients requiring immediate life-saving interventions and rapid transport.
Triage category for patients with major or multiple bone injuries, back injuries, or burns without airway problems.
The triage priority assigned to patients who need care but do not have immediately life-threatening conditions.
Triage category for patients with minor painful, swollen, deformed extremities, or minor soft tissue injuries.
The lowest triage priority assigned to walking wounded patients with minor injuries.
Triage category indicating death or injuries so severe that survival is highly unlikely given the available resources.
The most knowledgeable EMS provider arriving on-scene first who is responsible for assigning and coordinating triage.
A framework developed to provide an orderly means of communication, control, and coordination of emergency response resources.
The incident management sector responsible for safely removing patients from wreckage or entrapment at a mass casualty incident.
The incident management sector where emergency medical care is administered to patients after triage.
The incident management sector responsible for prioritizing and coordinating patient transport to appropriate medical facilities.
The designated incident sector where emergency vehicles wait until assigned a specific role or location.
The incident management sector responsible for the acquisition and distribution of necessary equipment and medical materials.
The central location at a mass casualty incident where overall direction and coordination of operations take place.
EMTs must always park the ambulance upwind and uphill from the hazardous materials incident at a safe distance.
EMTs must not enter a hazardous materials area unless specifically trained as a technician and equipped with SCBA.
A 24-hour resource center providing critical information for emergency response teams during hazardous materials incidents.
Permission for treatment given by a conscious, mentally competent adult who is informed of the procedures and risks.
The legal assumption that an unresponsive or mentally incompetent patient would agree to life-saving emergency intervention.
Unlawfully touching a patient or providing emergency care when the mentally competent patient has refused treatment.
The legal right of a mentally competent adult to withdraw from or decline emergency medical care and transportation.
The termination of patient care without ensuring the continuation of care at the same level or higher.
A deviation from the accepted standard of care that results in further injury or damages to the patient.
A contractual, legal, or ethical obligation requiring an EMT to provide emergency medical care to a patient.
The legal requirement prohibiting the release of patient history, assessment findings, and treatment without written permission.
Legal documents, such as a Do Not Resuscitate (DNR) order, indicating a patient's medical intervention preferences.
The proper use of the body to facilitate lifting and moving, minimizing the risk of injury to the EMT.
Using the legs instead of the back to lift, while keeping the patient's weight as close to the body as possible.
A lifting posture keeping the back locked into its normal curvature while using leg and thigh muscles.
A technique utilizing maximum hand force by wrapping all fingers and the palm completely around the object.
Immediately moving a patient due to scene hazards, risk of fire, or the inability to provide life-saving care.
Moving a patient in an emergency by pulling them lengthwise to provide as much spinal protection as possible.
Quickly moving a patient when there is an immediate threat to life, such as altered mental status or shock.
An urgent move used to quickly transition a critically injured sitting patient from a vehicle to a long backboard.
Carefully moving a patient when there is no immediate threat to life, allowing for complete assessment and immobilization.
A non-urgent move where two or three rescuers line up on one side to lift a supine patient.
A non-urgent carry technique where one rescuer lifts under the patient's arms and the other lifts behind the knees.
The most commonly used patient carrying device, designed to roll easily over smooth terrain and loaded into ambulances.
A device used to move conscious, non-spine-injured patients up or down stairs when a wheeled stretcher is impractical.
